schools.summary: Paul joins with himself the two who had been with him in founding the church: 'Silvanus (Silas) and Timotheus (Timothy)' — his fellow-labourers at Thessalonica (Acts 17), associated on a footing of near-equality, Paul not even adding his apostolic title; the church is addressed as 'in God the Father and in the Lord Jesus Christ' — its very being and life rooted in God and Christ, the Father and the Son named together as the sphere of its existence; 'grace (free favour) and peace (its fruit), from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ' (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Tertullian: the joint naming of God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ as the source of grace and peace witnesses to the Son's place beside the Father; the church lives in both.

themes: Paul, and Silvanus, and Timotheus, unto the church of the Thessalonians
which is in God the Father and in the Lord Jesus Christ
grace be unto you, and peace
the greeting from the three to the church in God and Christ

schools.summary: Paul opens, as is his custom, with thanksgiving: 'we give thanks to God always (constantly) for you all' — gratitude to God for the whole Thessalonian church, embracing every member; the thanksgiving continual, a settled, ongoing gratitude for the grace God had wrought in them; joined with intercession, 'making mention of you in our prayers' — remembering the Thessalonians by name before God, both thanking and asking on their behalf (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: We give thanks to God always for you all
making mention of you in our prayers
Paul's constant thanksgiving and prayer
remembering them by name

schools.summary: The famous triad of Christian graces, each shown by its fruit: 'work of faith (the work that faith produces, faith proving itself in action)'; 'labour of love (the toil that love prompts, love that spends itself in effort and self-denial)'; and 'patience (steadfast endurance) of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ' (the perseverance hope inspires, the steadfast waiting for Christ that sustains under trial) — faith, love, hope (cf. 1 Corinthians 13:13), all displayed 'in the sight of God' (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Cyril (with Wesley): the patience of hope is fixed on the Lord Jesus Christ and his coming; hope in him produces the steadfast endurance that marks the true believer under affliction.

themes: your work of faith, and labour of love, and patience of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ
the threefold grace: faith, love, hope
each grace shown by its fruit
the living religion of the model church

schools.summary: Gill and Calvin: 'election' is the eternal, gratuitous choice of God unto everlasting life, made in Christ before the world began, springing from God's sovereign will and free love, not from the foreseen faith, holiness, or merit of men; it is in itself hidden, but manifested by its marks — the effectual calling and the gospel's power (v.5). Calvin: gratuitous election must hold the first place, as that 'from which faith itself flows'; those err who, under pretext of faith, darken this first grace. Paul knew their election not by revelation but by its evidences.

Clarke and Wesley (with Ellicott): 'election' here is a gracious admission into present gospel privileges — the Gentiles called and chosen as the Jews once were (and now, in unbelief, rejected) — conditional, and able to be abused and finally forfeited, as the Jews' case proved. Clarke expressly denies that 'any irrespective, unconditional, eternal, and personal election to everlasting glory is meant'; Wesley: the election is 'through faith'; Ellicott: election implies nothing as to the final condition, and may be unsettled or made sure (2 Peter 1:10).

Barnes: the church as such (not necessarily every member, some being false professors) gave evidence that it was chosen of God, a true church; he rejects Clarke's notion that the Gentiles were chosen in the Jews' stead, since the Thessalonian church itself contained many Jews (Acts 17:4).

debates.question: What is the 'election of God' here?

sides.position: The eternal, unconditional, gratuitous choice of God unto everlasting salvation
A conditional election to present gospel privileges, which may be forfeited; not an election to glory
The church as such evidenced its election, without affirming it of every individual member

themes: Knowing, brethren beloved, your election of God
the assurance of their election
the crux of election
known by the gospel's power and fruit

schools.summary: The evidence of their election (v.4): 'our gospel came not unto you in word only (mere speech, bare preaching) but also in power' — accompanied by mighty effect, whether of miracles or, more likely, the converting power on hearts; 'and in the Holy Ghost, and in much assurance (full conviction, deep persuasion)' — the Spirit gave it efficacy and produced firm, settled conviction in the hearers; 'as ye know what manner of men we were among you for your sake' — Paul's own life and conduct confirming the gospel (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the gospel came not as bare, dead eloquence but as the living voice of God conjoined with the efficacy of the Spirit; Paul's integrity of conduct among them was itself part of the gospel's power and a proof of their calling.

themes: our gospel came not unto you in word only, but also in power, and in the Holy Ghost, and in much assurance
the gospel coming in power and the Spirit
full conviction wrought by the Spirit
Paul's conduct confirming the gospel

schools.summary: The Thessalonians 'became followers (mimetai — imitators) of us, and of the Lord' — they imitated Paul, and through him the Lord himself, copying the pattern of the gospel; they 'received the word in much affliction (tribulation, persecution)' — embracing the gospel amid suffering, for their conversion brought persecution from their countrymen (Acts 17:5-9), taking up the word at great cost (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Wesley: yet they received it 'with joy of the Holy Ghost' — a joy given by the Spirit that triumphed over their affliction; the persecution could not quench the gladness the Spirit wrought in them.

themes: ye became followers of us, and of the Lord
having received the word in much affliction, with joy of the Holy Ghost
receiving the word with joy in affliction
imitators of Paul and the Lord

schools.summary: The result of their faith and joyful endurance: 'ye were ensamples (typoi — patterns, models) to all that believe in Macedonia and Achaia' — their faith was so exemplary that they became a model for all the believers of the two great provinces of Greece; themselves recent converts and imitators (v.6), they so shone in faith and constancy that the imitators had become the imitated, their reputation spreading far beyond their own city (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: So that ye were ensamples to all that believe in Macedonia and Achaia
the Thessalonians becoming an example to all believers
the imitators become the imitated
a model through Macedonia and Achaia

schools.summary: The vivid figure: 'from you sounded out (exechetai — rang out, echoed forth, like a trumpet or thunderclap) the word of the Lord' — the gospel resounded from Thessalonica to the surrounding regions; 'not only in Macedonia and Achaia, but also in every place your faith to God-ward (toward God) is spread abroad' — the report had spread everywhere, far beyond Greece, Thessalonica being a great commercial city that sent the news abroad through its many travellers; so widely known that 'we need not to speak any thing' (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Cyril): so loud and far did the report of their faith ring out that the fame of their conversion preceded Paul; their faith toward God was itself a proclamation, needing no further telling.

themes: from you sounded out the word of the Lord not only in Macedonia and Achaia, but also in every place
your faith to God-ward is spread abroad
the word sounding out from them everywhere
their fame preceding Paul

schools.summary: Others everywhere 'shew (report, tell) of us what manner of entering in (what kind of reception, what success) we had unto you' — the report telling both of Paul's effective ministry and of the Thessalonians' response; 'how ye turned to God from idols' — the Thessalonians, largely Gentiles, abandoning idol-worship and turning to the true God; 'to serve (douleuein — serve as bondservants) the living and true God' — over against dead and false idols, now serving the God who lives and is real, conversion issuing in a new allegiance (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: how ye turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God
what manner of entering in we had unto you
turning from idols to serve the living God
conversion a new allegiance

schools.summary: The second mark of their conversion (with serving God, v.9): 'to wait for (anamenein — await, expect) his Son from heaven' — the Thessalonians lived in expectation of Christ's return, the forward-looking hope marking the believer; 'whom he (God) raised from the dead, even Jesus' — the resurrection the pledge that he will come again, the risen Jesus the one they await; 'which delivered (rescues, present-continuing) us from the wrath to come' — the coming wrath of God's judgment, from which Christ saves his people (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Cyril: their hope is a Saviour who delivers from the wrath to come; the waiting for the Son from heaven is no idle expectation but the confident hope of those rescued by him from condemnation.

themes: to wait for his Son from heaven, whom he raised from the dead, even Jesus
which delivered us from the wrath to come
waiting for God's Son from heaven
the Saviour who delivers from wrath
